name: AI-accessible internal data
question: In day-to-day work, AI tools available to the team have secure access to relevant internal code, documentation, standards, and operational information, and that context is routinely used to support onboarding, debugging, implementation, and code validation.
description: This capability means AI tools can securely use an organization's internal code, documentation, standards, and operational data when responding to software teams. In practice, the AI is not limited to a standalone prompt; it is given or retrieves the specific context needed for the task at hand. Teams see this in activities such as attaching relevant documents, referencing current code examples, and using outputs that reflect internal conventions and policies. The capability is visible when AI supports onboarding, debugging, implementation, and code validation by grounding responses in current internal information. It is about making internal knowledge accessible and relevant at the moment of work, rather than relying on generic model knowledge alone.

guidance.text: Stop relying on generic prompts for work that depends on internal knowledge. Pick one frequent task, such as onboarding, debugging, or API usage, and identify the internal documents, code examples, and standards it needs. Make those sources easy to find and confirm that the content is accurate before using it with AI. The next step is consistent manual use of a small set of trusted context sources.

Start standardizing how relevant context is gathered before asking AI for help. Create simple briefing packets or templates for recurring tasks that include the right docs, code snippets, and policy references. Remove outdated or conflicting materials from those packets so the AI is not fed bad examples. The next step is making context-rich prompts a repeatable team habit instead of an individual workaround.

Strengthen the shared library of reusable context templates and store it in version control so it can be reviewed and updated. Expand coverage beyond a few individuals by using the same approach for common onboarding, debugging, and implementation scenarios. Track which internal sources are most often attached and which ones are missing or unreliable. The next step is to pilot automated retrieval for one high-value use case instead of depending only on manual assembly.

Implement automated retrieval for a single use case where correct internal context matters and the source material is already trusted. Measure whether the system is pulling the right documents, how long retrieval takes, and how often requests fail or are denied. Avoid dumping large raw documents into the model; retrieve only the specific sections needed for the task. The next step is to prove reliable value and prepare the approach for broader use.

Broaden AI access to additional high-value internal sources, but curate them carefully so deprecated code and messy documents do not become examples the AI repeatedly uses. Enforce least-privilege access so responses are grounded in what the user is actually allowed to see. Review retrieval logs, source access rates, and query success patterns to remove weak sources and fix gaps. The next step is to operationalize the capability across more workflows with stronger data and API support.

Strengthen the underlying data delivery model by keeping critical documentation current and exposing trusted sources through robust internal integrations or APIs. Use results from pilots to secure continued ownership, maintenance, and investment so retrieval stays reliable as coverage expands. Measure effects on day-to-day work, such as faster onboarding, less time spent searching for information, and easier validation against internal standards. The next step is to protect quality at scale by continuously curating sources, relevance, and access controls.

Sustain performance by continuously refreshing the most important documentation, gold-standard repositories, and policy sources that feed the AI. Watch for regression signals such as higher retrieval latency, more failed queries, irrelevant context, or outputs that repeat deprecated patterns. Keep relevance-focused retrieval and least-privilege access in place so larger context windows do not become a substitute for disciplined context selection. Preserve the habit of measuring how often context is used and whether it continues to reduce information-seeking and verification effort.

name: Clear and communicated AI stance
question: It is clear in day-to-day work which AI tools and uses are allowed, which require specific controls, how the policy applies to this role, and where to get support or clarification when needed.
description: This capability describes whether an organization has a clear, practical, and well-communicated position on how AI should be used in software work. In practice, team members understand which tools are permitted, which uses are restricted, what safeguards apply, and how those rules relate to their specific role. The capability is visible when AI guidance is easy to find, consistently explained, and supported by clear channels for questions and experimentation. Its practical effect is to reduce uncertainty so people can use AI within defined boundaries instead of avoiding it or relying on unapproved tools.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where uncertainty is blocking work or pushing AI use into private, unreviewed practices. Create one visible source that states which tools are allowed, prohibited, or require approval, and make it easy for the team to find. Establish a clear contact point for questions so people do not have to guess. Move to the next level by replacing silence and inconsistency with basic, explicit rules.

Reduce contradictions by checking whether current guidance is understood the same way across engineering, security, legal, and product. Rewrite unclear policy language into practical examples tied to everyday software work, such as code generation, summarization, and handling internal data. Remove outdated or duplicate instructions that create confusion about what is actually permitted. Progress comes from making guidance concrete enough that team members can explain it without interpretation.

Strengthen role relevance by showing how the AI stance applies to common tasks performed by different software team members. Add examples of acceptable, prohibited, and controlled uses that reflect real work contexts rather than generic policy statements. Publish the approved-tool list, required guardrails, and escalation path in the same place. Advance by making the stance usable during normal delivery work, not just readable as a policy document.

Build regular communication into team operations so the stance is reinforced beyond its initial publication. Review whether meetings, onboarding, and internal documentation consistently point people to the same approved tools, boundaries, and support channels. Track recurring questions to find where the policy is still ambiguous or hard to apply. Improvement at this stage means shifting from passive availability to active, repeatable communication.

Create a structured feedback loop so software teams can raise edge cases, request tool reviews, and suggest updates based on real usage. Use those inputs to refine the approved-tool list, clarify guardrails, and close gaps between policy intent and delivery realities. Measure whether questions are becoming more specific and less basic, which indicates improving comprehension. Move higher by treating the stance as a maintained operating framework rather than a one-way announcement.

Focus on consistency and adaptation by reviewing the stance at a regular cadence and updating it as tools, risks, and workflows change. Check whether policy updates are timely without causing constant churn that makes teams stop paying attention. Strengthen cross-functional ownership so changes reflect engineering practice as well as legal, security, and IT requirements. Progress to the highest level comes when the stance stays stable enough to use and current enough to trust.

Sustain performance by continuing to monitor whether team members can quickly identify allowed tools, required controls, and the right path for clarification. Preserve the feedback loop, keep the guidance easy to find, and verify that updates remain grounded in actual software delivery work. Watch for regression signals such as renewed shadow AI use, repeated confusion about approvals, or policy changes that outpace team adoption. Maintain this level by keeping clarity, applicability, and communication aligned as AI practices evolve.

name: Code maintainability
question: Code in the wider codebase can be easily found, understood, reused, and changed when needed, and dependencies can be added or upgraded with little friction because versioning, review, testing, and traceability practices make breakages uncommon.
description: Code maintainability is the degree to which software teams can efficiently find, understand, reuse, and modify code across the codebase, including code owned by other teams when coordination is needed. In practice, this shows up when engineers can search for examples, propose changes through normal review workflows, and complete cross-team changes without unusual delay. It also includes dependency management: adding a library, upgrading versions, and tracing dependency use are routine and do not create frequent failures. Builds and tests make dependency changes visible and reproducible so teams can see when an update causes incompatibilities. Overall, the capability reflects whether everyday development work is supported by searchable code, manageable access and review paths, and stable dependency handling.

guidance.text: Start by making the codebase searchable in a shared version control platform and by showing where key services and libraries live. Stop depending on private copies, tribal knowledge, or ad hoc messages to find working examples. Introduce a standard path to propose changes outside direct ownership, such as pull requests with explicit approvers. Measure a simple baseline such as time to change another team's code or time to upgrade one dependency.

Expand read access so most source code can be viewed across the organization, with restrictions kept to genuinely sensitive areas. Clarify who approves changes in each area of the codebase and make that information easy to discover. Create a shared inventory of direct dependencies and the versions currently in use by each application. Review recent failed changes and dependency breakages to identify the biggest access or versioning bottlenecks.

Standardize how dependencies are declared and updated so teams follow the same approach rather than a mix of one-off methods. Record the exact dependency versions used to build each package or deployment so problems can be traced back quickly. Make builds reproducible by removing hidden machine-specific steps and verifying that the same inputs produce the same results. Use this traceability to shorten debugging when a dependency change causes failures.

Run automated tests on normal code changes and on dependency upgrades before changes are merged or released. Strengthen review workflows so cross-team changes can be proposed, reviewed, and audited without needing special side channels. Make it easier to search for examples and reusable components so teams can reuse existing code instead of reimplementing it. Track how often dependency updates fail and where manual coordination still slows changes.

Adopt a regular cadence for upgrading libraries instead of waiting for major issues or security events. Provide teams with known-good dependency versions and a quick way to see which systems consume each library. Reduce duplicate or unused code that makes search, reuse, and refactoring harder across the codebase. Measure how long it takes for a library update or cross-team code change to move from proposal to release.

Use tooling and process to support larger refactorings that touch multiple parts of the codebase without creating long coordination delays. Correlate dependency versions to deployed systems so vulnerable or outdated libraries can be found quickly and patched predictably. Test emergency change paths against the normal workflow and remove shortcuts that bypass review or validation without clear benefit. Focus measurement on propagation time for changes, upgrades, and vulnerability fixes so remaining bottlenecks are visible.

Protect the conditions that keep code maintainable: broad searchability, low-friction change proposals, stable dependency workflows, and reliable automated testing. Review exceptions such as restricted repositories, one-off dependency rules, or manual upgrade steps to prevent them from spreading. Continue monitoring indicators such as cross-team change lead time, dependency age, duplicate code, and time to patch vulnerable libraries. Periodically rehearse dependency and emergency change scenarios to catch regressions before they slow delivery or increase breakage.

name: Continuous delivery
question: Changes are kept in a deployable state through regular integration, reliable automated checks, and fast correction of failures, so software can be released to production or end users on demand with low operational disruption.
description: Continuous delivery means software and related production changes stay ready for release throughout development rather than only at the end of a project. Team members integrate work frequently, rely on fast and trustworthy feedback about quality and deployability, and treat failing builds or tests as immediate issues to resolve. The capability is visible when releases can happen on demand, including during normal business hours, without creating avoidable risk or disruption for users. It applies to more than application code, covering infrastructure, database, and other production changes that affect delivery. In practice, teams demonstrating this capability keep deployability visible to everyone and avoid long freezes, special release efforts, or heavy manual coordination before routine changes go live.

guidance.text: Start by making the current delivery path visible from version control to release. Include the people involved in development, testing, security, change management, and operations so the main waits, handoffs, and rework points are clear. Define what counts as a deployable state and make that definition visible to everyone working on the software. Treat failing builds, tests, or other signs that software is not deployable as the first problems to fix before taking on more change.

Put all production artifacts under version control, including code, configuration, environment scripts, and database change scripts. Increase check-in frequency and shorten branch lifetimes so work is merged into the mainline quickly instead of collecting on long-lived branches or freeze periods. Run a small set of quick automated checks on each check-in and share the results with the whole team. Begin measuring elapsed time from version control to release and note where work is repeatedly sent back because it was not done right the first time.

Automate the creation of canonical builds and packages so the same outputs move through later stages of delivery. Expand deployment automation for test and release environments so routine deployments do not depend on manual intervention. Ensure test data can be obtained when needed and does not restrict how many automated tests can run. When automated checks fail, pause feature work long enough to restore a deployable state instead of working around the failure.

Move from testing as a late phase to testing throughout the delivery lifecycle. Have developers and testers work side by side, aim for test feedback in less than ten minutes, and regularly review test suites so they keep finding real defects without unnecessary complexity. Integrate security into design and testing by using security reviews, involving security specialists in application design and demonstrations, and including security checks in automated tests. Make database changes visible to everyone in the delivery flow and manage them through version-controlled scripts.

Reduce dependencies that force teams to coordinate every release with other teams or services. Strengthen architecture and team boundaries so applications can be tested and deployed on demand without waiting for external orchestration, and improve code maintainability so developers can safely change shared code and upgrade dependencies. Add comprehensive monitoring and observability that covers predefined metrics and system state as experienced by users. Use proactive notifications so teams can detect and mitigate problems before they become visible failures.

Run value stream mapping across the full path from check-in to production and compare total elapsed time with the time when useful work is actually being done. Identify manual controls, approval layers, technical debt, and process returns that slow delivery, then remove or redesign the biggest bottlenecks first. Reserve team capacity for process simplification, architectural improvement, and capability development instead of relying on tooling alone. Make the regular release path good enough that urgent fixes can use the same process during normal business hours.

Sustain performance by continuously tracking lead time, change fail rate, time to restore service, release frequency, deployment pain, and time spent on rework or unplanned work. Keep improving test suites, observability, security practices, database change management, and architectural coupling as the system evolves. Repeat value stream mapping when delays or handoffs grow, and protect time for daily improvement work so manual controls and technical debt do not build up again. Use lessons from deployments, incidents, and system behavior to keep releases routine, low risk, and feasible during regular working hours.

name: Continuous integration
question: Code changes are merged into trunk or main at least daily, each commit automatically triggers a build and fast automated tests, build status is visible in shared channels, and broken builds are treated as urgent work until resolved.
description: Continuous integration means developers regularly merge small code changes into a shared main branch instead of letting branches drift for long periods. Each commit automatically produces a build and runs automated tests so problems are detected within minutes rather than after many changes accumulate. The build and test status is visible, making it easy to see whether the software is currently in a usable state. When a build fails, normal work pauses long enough to restore a working baseline quickly, often by fixing or reverting the change. In practice, this capability shows up in frequent integration, fast automated feedback, and a stable mainline that others can safely build on.

guidance.text: Document the current path from commit to usable build, including every manual handoff and missing script. Put all build and configuration inputs in version control so a build can be reproduced from the repository. Create one automated build that runs successfully at least once a day. Measure how many commits still require manual action before a build exists.

Add a small set of reliable automated unit and acceptance tests that cover high-value behavior. Configure the CI system so these checks run automatically instead of waiting for someone to start them. Make the current build result visible in a shared place such as the CI dashboard or team chat. Track which commits produce both a build and automated test run without manual intervention.

Reduce the size of code changes so commits can be integrated more often without large merge effort. Require every commit to trigger a build and a fast test suite, and remove slow or flaky checks from that quick path until feedback returns within a few minutes. Replace email-only notifications with a visible signal that people actually notice. Watch the percentage of daily successful automated builds and test runs to confirm the basic loop is dependable.

Shift development toward short-lived branches by merging into trunk or main at least daily. Break larger features into smaller increments that can be built, tested, and merged without waiting for the whole feature to finish. Make build packages repeatable, numbered, and authoritative so downstream environments use the same artifacts. Review branch age and merge frequency to catch drift before integration becomes painful.

Adopt a clear rule that a broken build interrupts normal work until the problem is fixed or the change is reverted. Measure the time between a build failure and restoration of a healthy build, and treat long delays as a process problem. Ensure current builds are consistently available for testers so exploratory testing can happen on recent code. Provide developers with at least daily feedback from acceptance and performance tests, not just unit tests.

Increase test coverage for new functionality so integration does not depend on manual checking of recent changes. Improve the reliability and speed of the suite by parallelizing tests, adding compute capacity, or moving longer-running checks into a separate pipeline while preserving a fast commit path. Confirm that quick automated tests still finish within about ten minutes and preferably within a few minutes. Review commit-triggered build rates, automated test execution rates, and daily success rates together to find weak points in the system.

Protect the habits that keep integration routine: small batches, daily merges to trunk or main, automatic builds and tests on every commit, and visible status for everyone. Continue to fix broken builds immediately, and remove new sources of delay such as manual steps, flaky tests, or hidden failures before they become normal. Keep monitoring restoration time, build and test automation coverage, tester build availability, and daily feedback from acceptance and performance tests. Use those signals to catch regression early and preserve a stable mainline that others can trust.

name: Customer feedback
question: Customer feedback is gathered regularly, reviewed before feature decisions, and used to adjust designs, prototypes, or delivered features, with success tracked through customer-centered measures such as satisfaction, adoption, retention, or continued use.
description: Customer feedback means product and feature decisions are grounded in evidence from real users rather than assumptions. In practice, teams gather feedback early and repeatedly, use it to confirm that a real problem exists, and adjust designs or features based on what they learn. The capability is visible when teams validate ideas before full implementation, track customer-centered measures such as satisfaction or adoption, and revisit those measures over time. It also applies to internal tools and services, not only external products. The focus is on whether delivered work solves user problems and is actually used, not only whether a requested feature was built as specified.

guidance.text: Begin collecting direct feedback from users instead of relying only on internal opinions or requirements documents. Pick one or two regular channels, such as interviews, support themes, or simple satisfaction measures, and review them on a fixed cadence. Before defining a feature, ask what user problem the work is meant to address and what evidence supports it. Stop treating delivery of the specified feature as the only signal of success.

Move feedback collection earlier in the lifecycle so it happens before solution details are locked in. Define a small set of customer-centered measures for the product, such as satisfaction, activation, continued use, or referral, and update them regularly. Make the latest feedback and metrics visible to the team so they can influence planning conversations. Reduce time spent refining features that have not been validated with users.

Use customer feedback to confirm that a real problem exists before committing to build. Introduce lightweight user research or prototypes for significant features so design choices can be checked before full implementation. When feedback reveals that the problem is different from the original assumption, update the scope instead of dismissing it as inconvenience or scope creep. Measure whether early changes improve the selected customer-centered metrics.

Create a consistent pattern in which each significant feature starts with problem discovery, then prototype or design validation, then implementation. Expect some proposed solutions to be wrong, and explicitly decide which ideas to stop, revise, or continue based on feedback. Track a few key measures that show whether users adopt, return to, or recommend the solution. Make feature changes traceable to specific research findings or customer signals.

Strengthen the loop between delivery and product decisions by reviewing feedback and customer measures after release, not just before build. Give the delivery team room to change designs or specifications when evidence shows the current approach is not solving the problem. Compare outcomes against the intended business or user result, not only against completion of the planned work. Use the results to choose the next iteration and remove features or changes that do not help.

Run customer feedback as a continuous operating rhythm rather than as occasional checkpoints. Combine regular satisfaction data, usage or retention measures, and targeted experiments to learn which changes improve outcomes. Iterate quickly on the smallest solution that addresses the confirmed problem and monitor whether the chosen metrics move in the expected direction. Look for patterns showing where feedback is late, ignored, or misinterpreted, and correct the process immediately.

Sustain performance by keeping user research, feedback review, and customer-centered measures embedded in normal delivery work. Continue validating major changes before full build, and re-check released features to confirm they still solve the problem as customer behavior changes. Protect the team's ability to revise or discard solutions when evidence shows a weak outcome. Watch for regression signals such as falling satisfaction, weak adoption, or feature work proceeding without recent customer input.

name: Database change management
question: To what extent are database schema changes discussed with production database owners, tracked visibly, kept in version control with related application code, and applied through automated migrations across development, test, and production environments?
description: This capability describes how database changes are handled as a normal part of software delivery rather than as a separate, opaque activity. In practice, schema changes are discussed with the people responsible for the production database before releases that depend on them. The work is visible to relevant teams, including which changes are pending, where they have been applied, and what their test or execution status is. Changes are stored in version control alongside related application code and executed as ordered migrations, so each environment has a known database state. The practical result is that database work can be traced, coordinated, and deployed without becoming a frequent source of delays or deployment problems.

guidance.text: List every current way database schema changes reach shared or production databases. Identify the people responsible for the production database and involve them before any application change that depends on a schema change. Create one visible place to track pending database changes and their current state. Stop relying on private messages, memory, or last-minute release discussions to coordinate database work.

Move database change definitions into version control instead of keeping them only in tickets, documents, or manual DBA steps. Store each change with the application code that depends on it so the relationship is explicit. Record whether each planned change has been tested and which shared environments it has reached. Stop treating database changes as a separate process with no shared source of truth.

Represent each schema change as an ordered migration script rather than as an informal request or one-off SQL execution. Give migrations unique sequence numbers so the execution order is unambiguous. Add a migrations table to every database instance to show exactly which changes have run there. Use the same migration set to create empty schemas for development and testing so environments start from a consistent baseline.

Introduce a migration tool or script that applies only the changes not yet run on a given database instance. Record the result of each execution by environment so teams can see what succeeded and what still needs attention. Make this status visible to both delivery teams and database administrators. Start measuring how often database work adds lead time or contributes to failed deployments.

Reduce release risk by separating application rollout from risky schema changes where possible. Use techniques such as online schema migration tools or parallel changes that keep old and new structures available during transition. Test each schema change against scrubbed production-like data, and use multi-node test setups when production is distributed. Review large tables for partitioning or archiving needs so migrations do not become excessively long or blocking.

Standardize the approach across teams and database owners so shared schemas are not changed through competing processes. For applications that share a database, agree on one version-controlled mechanism and rollout plan for all participating teams. Strengthen rollback safety by keeping old and new database structures synchronized until the application transition is complete. Track the percentage of changes executed through a fully automated push-button path and the share of incidents where database changes were a factor.

Keep every database schema change flowing through the same version-controlled, automated migration path across environments. Continue routine discussion with production database owners and preserve visible status for pending, tested, and applied changes so exceptions do not accumulate. Review failed changes, lead time impact, scheduled downtime cost, and any out-of-hours deployment work to catch regression early. Revalidate zero-downtime and production-like testing practices as architecture, scale, and data volumes change.

name: Deployment automation
question: Deployments to testing and production environments use the same automated process, rely on versioned scripts and configuration, run required deployment tasks and smoke tests, and can be triggered on demand without manual preparation or handoffs.
description: Deployment automation is the capability to move software changes into testing and production through a repeatable, automated process rather than a manually coordinated one. In practice, this means deployments use packaged build outputs, scripted environment setup, deployment steps, configuration, and a deployment test that checks whether the system is working after release. The same deployment process is used across environments, while environment-specific settings are kept separate from the deployable package. Team members with the right access can trigger deployments of a chosen version when needed, and the process leaves a record of what was deployed and how it ran. The capability is visible in everyday work when deployments are consistent, reproducible, and not dependent on informal knowledge or manual console actions.

guidance.text: Start by documenting the current deployment process step by step, including who performs each action and where manual decisions occur. Identify the smallest repeatable part of deployment that can be scripted first, such as copying artifacts, applying configuration, or restarting a service. Stop relying on undocumented handoffs or one-person knowledge as the normal way to release changes. Measure the current number of manual steps so progress toward the next level is visible.

Automate the most error-prone and frequently repeated deployment steps, even if the full process is not yet automated. Store deployment scripts and environment-specific configuration in version control so changes are visible and repeatable. Start using build artifacts produced by the CI process as the input to deployments instead of rebuilding for each environment. Track where deployment delays still occur and which manual approvals or preparations are blocking on-demand execution.

Extend automation beyond a few scripts into a connected deployment flow that prepares environments, deploys packages, applies required deployment tasks, and runs a basic deployment test. Use the same package in multiple environments and keep environment-specific settings separate from the package itself. Reduce cases where deployment requires someone to log into consoles and click through manual steps. Strengthen the process by making each automated step easier to rerun after failure.

Standardize the deployment process so testing and production follow the same automated path rather than different team-specific methods. Make it possible for authorized team members to deploy a selected artifact version to an environment without waiting for someone else to prepare it manually. Add records of which build is in each environment and keep deployment output for later review. Measure how often deployments still depend on coordination across teams or environment-specific exceptions.

Improve reliability by making deployment steps idempotent, so rerunning them does not create inconsistent states after partial failure. Remove unnecessary ordering constraints where possible and design services to tolerate temporarily unavailable dependencies rather than failing uncontrollably. Automate related tasks such as configuration generation, middleware setup, and database migrations within the deployment flow. Review failure cases from recent deployments and simplify the process instead of adding more fragile workaround logic.

Work on reducing architectural dependencies that force tightly synchronized, multi-service deployment orchestration. Strengthen backward compatibility and independent deployability so one service can be released without requiring lock-step changes elsewhere. Ensure environments can be recreated from version-controlled information, improving repeatability and disaster recovery readiness. Measure the percentage of the deployment pipeline that is automated and the time lost to waiting or stalled steps.

Sustain the capability by regularly reviewing deployment logs, automation coverage, manual step counts, and pipeline delays for signs of regression. Keep scripts, configuration, and environment recreation data current in version control as systems, platforms, and dependencies evolve. Protect the use of the same automated deployment process across environments, and challenge any new manual exception before it becomes standard practice. Continue simplifying the deployment path so automation remains understandable, repeatable, and easy for both development and operations to maintain.

name: Documentation quality
question: Project documentation is clear, easy to find, and kept current enough that team members can rely on it during development, changes, and operations without needing repeated verbal clarification or extra effort to confirm what is accurate.
description: Documentation quality refers to project documentation being understandable, easy to locate, and dependable enough to support everyday software work. In practice, people can find the information they need without excessive searching and can use it confidently during development, delivery, and operational tasks. The capability is visible when important technical knowledge is written down, kept current, and maintained as work changes. It captures whether documentation consistently supports how technical practices are carried out, rather than relying on informal memory or repeated verbal explanations. This survey item is meant to assess those observable documentation habits and conditions.

guidance.text: Key technical information is mostly missing, outdated, or trusted only through word of mouth. Start by documenting the few workflows, decisions, and system details that people need most often in one shared location. Remove or clearly mark obsolete pages so they stop undermining reliability. Treat writing and updating documentation as part of the work, not as something separate from software delivery.

Some documentation exists, but it is scattered and difficult to find when work is underway. Create a clear home for project documentation and move the most used content there so people know where to start. Rewrite the highest-use pages in plain, direct language so the purpose and current state are easier to understand. Check whether common tasks can be completed from the documentation without asking for basic clarification.

Documentation covers several topics, but quality varies and important gaps remain. Set a simple standard for what good documentation must provide: clear wording, predictable location, and information that matches current practice. Link related documents so people can move from overview material to detailed instructions without guesswork. Keep a short list of pages that are often questioned, hard to find, or no longer trusted, and fix those first.

Documentation usually helps with daily work, but updates still lag behind changes in the software or process. Add documentation review to the normal completion of changes so important details are updated at the same time as the work. Check pages not just for correctness, but also for whether they are easy to locate and easy to understand. Use recurring questions and failed searches as signals that documentation needs revision.

Documentation is generally clear and dependable, and people use it regularly during software work. Strengthen this level by measuring whether important content is current, easy to find, and actually used in real tasks. Close the remaining gaps where people still rely on private notes, side channels, or repeated explanations. Use any available documentation guidelines, training, or documentation-focused support to improve consistency across contributors.

Documentation is actively maintained and supports a wide range of technical practices. Move toward the highest level by assigning explicit ownership for high-value documents and reviewing them at regular points in the work cycle. Compare documentation against recent changes and recurring questions to catch drift early. Consolidate duplicate material so there is one reliable source for each important topic.

Documentation is clear, findable, reliable, and maintained as a normal part of software development. Sustain this level by continuing to update documentation whenever technical practices, tools, or workflows change, and by removing content that no longer reflects reality. Periodically confirm that critical information can still be found quickly and used with confidence. Watch for regression signals such as repeated clarification requests, duplicate notes, or declining use of the main documentation source.

name: Empowering teams to choose tools
question: People doing the software delivery work can choose tools and technologies that fit the team’s tasks within a shared baseline, and exceptions are documented, reviewed, and supported with clear ownership for operating and maintaining those choices.
description: This capability is about giving the people who build, test, deploy, and operate software meaningful influence over the tools and technologies they use in their daily work. In practice, teams are not locked into a single mandated toolset for every situation, but they also do not choose tools without boundaries or accountability. A shared baseline of approved tools exists, and teams can use a different option when there is a clear reason and the added support burden is understood. Observable practices include periodic review of the baseline, discussion of new technologies, and documented exceptions when a project uses tools outside the standard set. The central idea is that tool choices are informed by the work being done and by the team’s responsibility to support the resulting system.

guidance.text: Tool decisions are largely imposed or happen informally without any clear process. Start by identifying where teams currently have no practical input and where untracked tool variation already exists. Create a simple baseline list of approved tools and name who can propose changes or exceptions. Make sure any nonstandard tool already in use is recorded with its purpose and owner.

Some discussion about tools happens, but choices are still mostly dictated or approved inconsistently. Introduce a defined exception path so teams can request a different tool with a short written rationale, expected benefit, and support implications. Stop allowing undocumented deviations that later complicate maintenance and troubleshooting. Begin reviewing whether the baseline covers most common development, testing, deployment, monitoring, and data needs.

Teams can sometimes choose tools, but the baseline is narrow or outdated and exceptions are slow or unclear. Expand the baseline with input from developers, testers, operators, and product representatives so it better reflects actual work. Add a recurring review point, such as in retrospectives or periodic tooling reviews, to evaluate whether current tools are effective. Track each approved exception in project documentation so future maintainers can understand what was chosen and why.

A baseline exists and exceptions are possible, but evaluation of tools is still irregular and experimentation is limited. Strengthen the process by scheduling regular opportunities to assess the current stack against requirements and identify gaps. Run small, practical experiments for promising tools before wider adoption, using a meaningful slice of real work rather than opinion alone. Measure not only expected benefits but also support, licensing, infrastructure, hiring, and communication costs.

Teams usually have meaningful choice, and new tools are considered with some structure, but learning is not yet broadly shared. Create regular forums where teams present new technologies, lessons from experiments, and hidden costs found during use. Strengthen cross-team visibility so useful exceptions can inform future baseline updates instead of remaining isolated local decisions. Stop treating tool selection as a one-time decision and make re-evaluation part of normal delivery work.

Tool choice is well supported, and teams usually balance autonomy with ownership, but consistency depends on continued discipline. Tighten the feedback loop between experiments, exception documentation, and baseline changes so proven choices become easier for other teams to adopt. Check whether different tool chains are creating avoidable communication or integration friction across shared systems. Use team feedback directly to confirm that perceived choice reflects real day-to-day practice rather than policy alone.

Teams consistently choose tools that fit their work within a well-understood baseline, and exceptions are handled with clear ownership and documentation. Sustain this by continuing periodic reviews of the baseline, maintaining lightweight exception processes, and preserving regular discussion of new technologies. Guard against regression by watching for two failure modes: renewed top-down mandates that remove practical choice, and uncontrolled tool sprawl that increases fragility and maintenance cost. Keep asking team members whether they genuinely feel empowered, because perceived autonomy is the clearest check that the capability is still present.

name: Flexible infrastructure
question: Teams can provision, change, and scale infrastructure and environments on demand through version-controlled, automated mechanisms, with clear usage visibility, without relying on manual tickets, email requests, or prolonged waiting for routine configuration and deployment needs.
description: Flexible infrastructure means technical environments can be provisioned, changed, and accessed when needed without depending on provider-side human intervention. In practice, teams experience this as self-service access to environments and configuration through automated mechanisms rather than ticket queues or email handoffs. The capability also includes elastic scaling, shared platform resources, and visibility into infrastructure usage and operating cost. Day-to-day work is supported by version-controlled, automated infrastructure changes that leave clear records and fit security and governance requirements. The practical test is whether teams can obtain and adjust infrastructure quickly enough to support reliable releases, availability, and routine operational work.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where infrastructure work depends on tickets, emails, or direct intervention from another team. List the most common requests, such as environment creation, configuration changes, and deployment-related access, and measure how long each takes from request to completion. Stop treating these delays as normal background work and make them visible in team reporting. Choose one high-frequency request and define a simple self-service target for it.

Reduce reliance on manual handoffs for at least one recurring infrastructure task. Move the relevant configuration into version control so changes can be reviewed and repeated instead of recreated manually each time. Add a basic automated path for provisioning or changing that resource through the platform API. Measure how often the team still falls back to tickets or ad hoc administrator action.

Expand self-service from a single use case to a small set of routine team needs, such as test environments, standard configuration changes, and deployment prerequisites. Strengthen the automation so requests can be completed on demand without waiting for a person to execute them. Ensure every automated action produces a visible record of what changed and when. Stop allowing undocumented manual changes that bypass the version-controlled path.

Integrate security and governance into the automated workflow instead of handling them as separate manual gates. Require infrastructure changes to pass through defined review or approval steps in version control, with clear traceability from request to applied change. Start measuring end-to-end change time for infrastructure updates, not just execution time after approval. Add basic usage and cost reporting so teams can see what resources are being consumed.

Broaden the approach so infrastructure can scale outward or inward as demand changes, rather than being sized and adjusted manually. Strengthen tagging, ownership, or chargeback-style mechanisms so usage data is connected to the relevant team, product, or environment. Replace long-lived, manually tuned hosts where possible with reproducible provisioning patterns. Measure whether teams can obtain environments and make routine changes fast enough to support frequent, reliable delivery.

Use the automation path as the normal way of working across most environments, not just for selected services. Review exceptions regularly and remove remaining cases where infrastructure changes still require specialist intervention without a clear reason. Instrument the process to track request volume, change lead time, provisioning speed, scaling behavior, and cost visibility in a consistent way. For systems that still block these outcomes, prioritize architectural changes that better fit cloud-native operation.

Sustain the capability by continuously checking that self-service, elasticity, traceability, and measured usage still hold in daily work. Watch for regression signals such as growing ticket queues, more direct production changes, poor cost attribution, or increasing numbers of long-lived manually maintained hosts. Keep platform workflows, approval rules, and logging aligned with current security and regulatory needs while preserving fast automated execution. Regularly review outcome measures such as provisioning time, infrastructure change time, availability support, and cost visibility so the capability remains effective as systems evolve.

name: Generative organizational culture
question: Members of this software team openly surface problems, share responsibility across functions, work with adjacent roles, examine failures to improve the system, and act on new ideas in everyday delivery work.
description: Generative organizational culture describes a high-trust, performance-oriented environment where useful information reaches the people who need it in time to act. In practice, software teams cooperate across roles, share responsibility for delivery and operation, and treat raising bad news as a normal part of the work. Problems and failures are examined to understand causes and improve the system rather than to blame individuals. Boundaries between functions are actively bridged so decisions and risks are visible across the software delivery lifecycle. New ideas are welcomed and turned into experiments, process changes, or product improvements.

guidance.text: Start by making it safe to report problems, failures, and risks without blame. Set a simple rule that failures and bad news are discussed to understand causes, not to identify a person at fault. Begin a recurring review where issues, blockers, and risks are surfaced early and tracked to follow-up actions.

Stop treating delivery, quality, security, and operations as separate concerns owned by isolated roles. Define a small set of shared responsibilities for building, deploying, and maintaining the product, and make those responsibilities visible in routine work. Add the missing functional perspectives to planning and review discussions so questions are answered by the people involved.

Create more regular cross-functional collaboration instead of relying on handoffs between specialized groups. Include representatives from development, quality, operations, security, and other relevant functions in planning and delivery work. Ensure developers also share responsibility for maintaining code in production so information from operations changes everyday engineering decisions.

Make information flow faster and more usable across boundaries. Run blameless postmortems after failures, capture system-level causes, and turn the findings into visible changes in process, code, or automation. Add one bridging practice such as involving operations throughout the delivery lifecycle, using ChatOps, or creating regular informal conversations with functions that are poorly understood.

Strengthen routines that encourage people to bring bad news early and share risks before issues grow. Check whether responsibility is actually shared for quality, availability, reliability, and security, and correct gaps where ownership remains narrow. Protect time for experimentation so new ideas can become small trials, internal demos, process changes, or product improvements.

Measure the culture directly with short survey items about seeking information, shared responsibilities, cross-functional collaboration, learning from failure, and welcoming new ideas. Review the results with the team, identify the lowest-scoring behaviors, and assign concrete follow-up actions with owners and dates. Check that managers and leaders are providing the support needed so these practices continue beyond individual effort.

Sustain performance by keeping cross-functional cooperation, shared responsibility, blameless inquiry, and experimentation part of normal delivery work rather than occasional initiatives. Watch for regression signals such as ignored bad news, renewed silos, blame after failures, or resistance to novel ideas, and address them quickly. Repeat the survey periodically and use the results to refresh collaboration practices, automation, and connections across functions as the work changes.

name: Healthy data ecosystems
question: In day-to-day work, the data needed for development and delivery is easy to find, trusted to be accurate and current, documented near its source, and available through consistent, approved paths instead of being scattered across separate tools or silos.
description: Healthy data ecosystems describes a working environment where internal data is reliable, easy to access, and connected across sources instead of fragmented in isolated systems. In practice, software teams can quickly find the data they need, trust that it is accurate and current, and understand what it represents. Important datasets have clear ownership, documentation, and metadata, so both people and tools can use them with less guesswork. Access happens through defined pathways rather than ad hoc requests or hidden copies. This capability reflects whether data supports daily development work as a usable, shared resource rather than a scattered by-product of separate applications.

guidance.text: Start with one high-value service or application and list the internal data sources needed to build, test, operate, or support it. Assign a named owner or steward for the most critical dataset and record where it lives, who grants access, and how long access usually takes. Remove obviously outdated copies or unofficial side sources from the workflow so work begins from a smaller, known set of data assets.

Create a local, versioned description of the data in the repository or service README, including what data is created, consumed, and where the authoritative source resides. Define a simple approved path for obtaining and using that data instead of relying on chat messages or personal memory. Track one basic measure, such as time to find a definitive answer or time to gain access, so the main friction point becomes visible.

Focus on one important workflow where data is still scattered across tools or systems. Consolidate or federate the key sources for that workflow into a clearer single view, and add basic tagging or context so relevant data can be found faster. Review recent cases where work stalled because data could not be found or used, and remove the specific silo or handoff that caused the delay.

Introduce automated validation for the selected datasets, covering accuracy, completeness, and timeliness in the same way automated tests protect code. Make validation results visible in normal delivery or operational workflows so problems are discovered before they spread into downstream work. Begin tracking data-related bugs or production incidents and use that evidence to choose the next improvement target.

Expand access through a secure paved road that lets teams and tools discover approved datasets, metadata, and usage rules without repeated manual intervention. Strengthen ownership by making stewards responsible for metadata quality, access policies, and correction of recurring issues. Review whether definitive answers can usually be found within about an hour and whether access delays are shrinking for the targeted domains.

Apply the same practices across multiple critical data domains so the experience is consistent instead of limited to one pilot area. Regularly inspect freshness indicators, access times, and data incidents to detect regressions and gaps between systems. Challenge tool-specific structures that recreate silos, and adjust integrations or schemas to preserve a unified view across sources.

Sustain performance by keeping data ownership, local documentation, automated checks, and governed access current as systems and teams change. Watch leading indicators such as access delay, freshness, and data-related incidents so fragmentation or quality decay is detected early. Review new tools and workflows against the shared data architecture to prevent the return of isolated copies, hidden pathways, or undocumented sources.

name: Job satisfaction
question: Team members have the tools, resources, and autonomy needed to do their work effectively, and the work assigned is meaningful, uses their expertise, and allows judgment to shape day-to-day decisions and improvement efforts.
description: This capability describes whether people can do software work that is both effective and worthwhile. In practice, it shows up when team members have the tools, environments, time, and learning resources needed to complete their work without unnecessary friction. It also depends on work being meaningful and aligned with the skills and judgment of the people doing it. When this capability is present, people are more likely to contribute engagement, creativity, and strong thinking in everyday delivery work. It is best understood through the experiences and perceptions of team members rather than through system metrics alone.

guidance.text: Start by identifying the most basic blockers that prevent work from being done, such as missing tools, poor access to environments, or unclear task ownership. Ask team members directly where work feels pointless, frustrating, or disconnected from their expertise, and capture specific examples. Stop treating dissatisfaction as an individual issue when the same problems affect multiple people. Create a short list of concrete fixes that can be completed quickly before asking people to reassess.

Remove recurring obstacles that waste time every week, especially gaps in tooling, access, or support for learning new skills. Review how work is assigned and stop routinely giving tasks that ignore people’s expertise or leave no room for judgment. Introduce a regular check-in that asks whether people have what they need to do meaningful work. Use the answers to make visible follow-up actions rather than collecting feedback without response.

Strengthen the basics by making needed tools and resources consistently available across the team, not only when problems escalate. Give clearer ownership of work that matters and connect tasks to meaningful outcomes so people can see why the work matters. Increase opportunities for practitioners to apply judgment in how work is executed. Track whether previously reported blockers are actually removed and whether similar issues keep returning.

Make practitioner input a normal part of tool and workflow decisions so the team can shape how work gets done. Reserve time and resources for improvement work, especially when changes in technology, process, or skills are underway. Strengthen access to learning resources, training, or technical support when new capabilities are expected. Check whether meaningful work and practical support are improving together, rather than addressing only one of them.

Refine work allocation so people regularly take on tasks that use their expertise and require thoughtful judgment, not only execution. Strengthen support during change by planning time for adaptation when new tools, automation, or delivery practices are introduced. Measure job satisfaction directly through team feedback and review patterns in the responses for specific operational causes. Use those findings to adjust resources, work design, and improvement priorities in the next cycle.

Protect the conditions that make the capability visible in daily work by reviewing whether teams still control the tools and resources they depend on. Watch for regression during reorganizations or delivery pressure, when meaningful work and practitioner judgment are often reduced first. Strengthen continuous improvement by keeping time available for process changes and by building skill in improvement work itself. Compare survey feedback over time to confirm that support, meaningful work, and effective execution remain aligned.

Sustain performance by continuing to ask team members directly about their ability to do meaningful work with the right tools, resources, and authority. Keep tool choice, learning support, and improvement capacity under regular review so they do not erode as priorities change. Guard against regression by treating new friction, reduced autonomy, or less meaningful task allocation as early warning signs. Maintain visible follow-through on feedback so strong job satisfaction remains an active team practice rather than an assumption.

name: Learning culture
question: Learning is treated as a normal part of software work through protected time or budget, regular knowledge sharing, support for trying new ideas, and blameless reflection on failures to improve systems and ways of working.
description: This capability concerns whether learning is treated as a normal and worthwhile part of software work rather than as an optional extra. In practice, it can be seen in dedicated time and budget for training, space to explore ideas, and access to continued education. It is also visible when people regularly share knowledge through talks, write-ups, study groups, or similar forums. A learning culture is present when experimentation is supported, reasonable failure is used to improve systems, and reviews after problems are handled without blame. For software project teams, the question is whether everyday team and organizational behavior consistently supports learning through formal training, informal exploration, knowledge sharing, and reflection after failure.

guidance.text: Learning is largely absent from normal delivery work. There is little or no protected time, budget, or structure for training, sharing knowledge, or exploring new ideas, and failure is more likely to be avoided or hidden than discussed. Start by putting one visible practice in place, such as a recurring knowledge-sharing session or a small protected block for learning. Use the next incident or failed change to run a blameless review and capture a small number of system or process changes.

Learning happens occasionally, but it depends on individual effort rather than team or organizational support. Useful activities such as training, post-incident reflection, or sharing takeaways occur inconsistently and are easy to drop when delivery pressure rises. Move toward the next level by assigning time on the calendar for learning, setting aside a modest budget, and making one person responsible for maintaining a simple cadence of sharing. Record outcomes from training, talks, or reviews so learning is visible and not forgotten.

Some repeatable learning practices exist, but coverage is uneven and the practices are still fragile. Teams may run occasional brownbags or post-mortems, yet informal exploration and continued education still compete with feature work and are not reliably supported. Progress by making key practices routine: schedule regular sharing sessions, define access to training resources, and protect some time for exploration. Measure whether sessions happen, whether people participate, and whether review actions are completed.

Learning is supported in several visible ways and is no longer purely ad hoc. There may be a training budget, regular sessions for sharing knowledge, safer discussion of failure, and some support for conferences, certifications, or study groups. Strengthen this level by widening participation so learning is not concentrated in a few people or topics. Make learning materials, notes, and review outcomes easy to find, and check that takeaways lead to concrete changes in systems or processes.

Learning is becoming embedded in day-to-day team practice. People regularly bring back ideas from external training or conferences, knowledge is shared across the team, and blameless reviews are used to improve how work is done rather than to assign fault. Move higher by linking learning activities more directly to operational follow-through, such as tracked actions from post-mortems or experiments inspired by new knowledge. Strengthen study groups, presentation rotation, and time for side exploration so learning remains active and broad.

Learning is clearly treated as an investment and is supported across multiple channels. Formal education, informal exploration, regular knowledge exchange, and blameless learning from failure are all part of normal work, and people can point to changes that resulted from them. Advance by protecting these practices against schedule pressure and by checking for weak spots such as low participation, limited topic diversity, or follow-up actions that stall. Use periodic surveys or simple indicators to confirm that people still feel learning is encouraged and supported.

Learning is consistently sustained as a strategic part of software delivery. Time, budget, continued education, information sharing, and safe reflection after failure are all stable, expected, and visible in everyday work. Sustain this level by monitoring participation, reviewing whether post-mortem actions and learning commitments are completed, and refreshing learning formats so they remain useful. Avoid regression by protecting learning capacity during deadlines, leadership changes, or incidents, and by watching for signs that experimentation or open discussion is becoming less safe.

name: Loosely coupled teams
question: Day-to-day work on this team can be changed, tested, and deployed with little dependence on other teams, using isolated testing, stable service contracts, and releases that do not require coordinated changes, approvals, or downtime.
description: Loosely coupled teams work in a system and team structure that lets them make, test, and release changes without frequent dependence on other teams. In practice, this means most work can be completed without extensive cross-team coordination, shared approval bodies, or synchronized releases. Services are designed so they can be tested in isolation, often through test doubles and contract tests rather than scarce integrated environments. Teams can deploy during normal business hours with little disruption, and changes in one area do not routinely trigger cascading work in others. The capability is visible when boundaries, interfaces, and deployment processes support independent delivery of value.

guidance.text: Begin by making the current coupling visible. Measure how often work requires outside approval, shared test environments, coordinated releases, and waiting on other teams. Stop bundling unrelated changes into big-bang deployments whenever a smaller release is possible. Pick one service or feature boundary that a single team can own end to end and document its external dependencies.

Choose one recurring dependency that slows delivery and reduce it first. Add stubs, mocks, or fakes for one external service so functional validation can happen without a full integrated environment. Define a clear interface for that dependency and record handoffs and wait times around it. Separate release planning for that component so it no longer has to ship only when several services change together.

Expand isolated testing from a local workaround into a normal team practice. Add contract tests for the most important service interactions and introduce versioned APIs where interface changes are frequent. Track what percentage of deployments still must be coordinated with other services and review the causes after each release. Use that data to remove one approval step, shared environment dependency, or cross-team handoff at a time.

Make independent delivery the default for a defined service boundary. Align product, development, testing, and operations responsibilities so the team can make routine design and release decisions without handing work off. Use backward-compatible API changes and a clear deprecation window so consumers are not forced into simultaneous updates. Adopt deployment patterns such as rolling or blue/green releases to make daytime production deployments routine and low risk.

Focus on the dependencies that still create unplanned work. Measure how often upstream failures degrade the service, how often upstream changes force local changes, and how often contract tests fail. Strengthen observability and API usage analytics so teams can see who depends on older versions and plan migrations before breaking changes. Remove remaining manual bottlenecks in testing or deployment that many teams rely on.

Use the architecture as a living system that is improved iteratively, not through occasional large redesigns. For new work, enforce service boundaries around a single business capability and require that each service be buildable, testable, and deployable on its own. Standardize the basics needed to operate many services effectively, such as service discovery, quotas or throttling, and a debuggable runtime. Review coordination hours, wait times, coordinated deployment rates, and isolated test coverage regularly to catch weak points before they spread.

Sustain the capability by treating independent testability and deployability as non-negotiable design constraints. Keep service contracts, contract tests, and API versioning discipline current, including limiting supported versions and retiring deprecated ones on a clear timeline. Watch for regressions in coordinated releases, downtime, approval rates, and cross-team wait times, and act when any of them trend upward. Continue evolving the architecture as requirements change so small teams can keep delivering quickly and safely.

name: Monitoring and observability
question: Day to day, the team can detect service problems early, understand customer impact, use metrics, logs, and traces to investigate production behavior, and trace issues across services and infrastructure without relying only on predefined alerts or dashboards.
description: Monitoring and observability means a software team can see the health of running systems, understand customer impact, and investigate production behavior using signals such as metrics, logs, and traces. In practice, teams track service health, key business and system indicators, and symptoms that suggest degradation or outages. They can inspect live systems to diagnose issues, including unexpected interactions across services and infrastructure layers. The capability also includes exploring patterns that were not predefined, not just reviewing fixed dashboards or alerts. In day-to-day work, this is visible when engineers use shared tools and data to detect problems, follow requests through the system, and understand what changed or failed.

guidance.text: Start by making production system health visible with a small set of shared checks for service availability, resource status, and customer-facing failures. Add basic metrics and logs for the main service path so problems can be detected without waiting for user reports. Define where this data is viewed and who uses it during normal delivery work. Measure whether incidents are first noticed by monitoring or by customers, and reduce that gap.

Strengthen the signal quality by focusing monitoring on symptoms that affect users instead of trying to alert on every possible internal cause. Add simple blackbox checks that exercise important user journeys or external APIs on a schedule. Store probe results and enough context to understand failures later. Measure how often alerts lead to immediate action versus being ignored or closed without action.

Expand beyond basic checks by instrumenting important parts of the application so metrics and logs expose internal state during normal operation and failure. Cover the most common failure paths, key dependencies, and major production workflows first. Make the collected data available to developers, not only to a specialized operations function. Track which incidents still require guesswork because the necessary signal is missing, then instrument those areas next.

Improve investigation speed by adding traces or equivalent request-correlation mechanisms across services, queues, and infrastructure layers. Connect application signals with underlying platform signals so engineers can move from a failing request to likely contributing components. Organize dashboards and views around troubleshooting tasks rather than around a fixed "perfect" display. Measure time to detect and time to restore, and identify where missing correlation slows both.

Treat monitoring configuration as an actively maintained part of the delivery system. Put alert rules, dashboards, and related configuration under version control, review changes, and deploy them through automation so updates are consistent and fast. Use postmortems or retrospectives to add or refine monitoring after failures and unexpected side effects. Measure how often monitoring changes are made and how quickly they reach production.

Tune the operating model so alerts are actionable, routed clearly, and supported by useful runbooks and visualizations. Reduce noisy or unactionable alerts, review suppressed alerts carefully, and separate operational views from executive or product reporting views. Check whether alerts are acknowledged within the expected time and whether out-of-hours pages indicate missing leading indicators. Strengthen usability by making it easy to move from an alert to the relevant dashboard, trace, and playbook.

Sustain performance by regularly reviewing whether monitoring still reflects current architecture, dependencies, and user-impacting risks as the system changes. Keep instrumentation, alerting, and correlation current through normal development work rather than periodic cleanups. Watch for regression through measures such as false positives, false negatives, acknowledgement time, MTTD, MTTR, and customer impact. Preserve broad developer access and proficiency so the capability does not collapse back into a single expert function.

name: Monitoring systems to inform business decisions
question: Monitoring data from application performance, infrastructure, and delivery activities is routinely collected, made visible in useful forms, shared with relevant roles, and used in meetings, reports, or planning discussions to guide product and operational decisions.
description: Monitoring in this capability means collecting meaningful data about applications, infrastructure, and work across the delivery pipeline, then turning that data into information people can use. In practice, teams make monitoring visible beyond operations so developers, product roles, and others can understand what is happening in the system. The capability is demonstrated when data is shared in dashboards, reports, or meetings in forms that are timely, accurate, relevant, and easy to interpret. It also includes giving context around the numbers so people can judge whether values are expected, changing, or different from past results. The practical goal is not data collection by itself, but using monitored information to guide decisions about the product, technology, and business.

guidance.text: Start by identifying a small set of key signals from application performance and infrastructure instead of waiting for outages to reveal problems. Set up one shared place to capture and view that data, even if it is a simple spreadsheet or basic dashboard. Remove measures that are not tied to a clear decision or audience. The immediate goal is to move from reactive detection to visible, usable information.

Expand monitoring beyond one or two components so it covers important points in development work, testing, QA, and operations. Choose metrics that match the function being observed and matter to the business, rather than collecting everything available. Reduce alert noise and duplicate reporting so attention stays on the few signals that indicate meaningful change. This creates a broader but still focused view of the delivery pipeline.

Begin turning collected data into simple visualizations or reports that different roles can actually use. Bring the information into recurring discussions such as incident reviews, delivery check-ins, or planning conversations. When presenting numbers, explain whether they are high or low, expected or unexpected, and how they compare with recent history. This helps monitoring move from raw data toward informed interpretation.

Use monitoring information consistently in decisions about releases, operational changes, and product priorities rather than only for technical troubleshooting. Share relevant operations insights upstream with development and product roles so lessons do not stay isolated in one part of the pipeline. Review whether technology or infrastructure changes are affecting results in ways that are not obvious from a single metric. The next step is to make monitoring data part of normal decision flow across roles.

Strengthen the link between monitored signals and specific business or delivery decisions by defining where each metric is reviewed and what actions it can trigger. Match the presentation format to the audience, such as real-time views for operational work and periodic summaries for longer-term decisions. Check for gaps in coverage across the value chain and close them deliberately rather than adding more data everywhere. This improves usefulness without creating unnecessary volume.

Treat learnings from production issues, deployment problems, and customer usage patterns as inputs for other teams that build, test, or plan the product. Compare current data with historical reports so changes can be explained rather than merely noticed. Watch for local optimizations by asking whether an improvement in one service or area benefits the broader system. At this stage, monitoring should support shared understanding across the organization, not isolated reporting.

Sustain performance by regularly confirming that monitoring data remains relevant, timely, accurate, and easy for each audience to understand. Keep pruning metrics, alerts, and reports that no longer inform decisions so the system does not drift into noise. Continue sharing context with the numbers and refreshing how insights are fed into meetings, briefings, and cross-team communication. The focus is to preserve decision usefulness and prevent regression into reactive or fragmented monitoring.

name: Pervasive security
question: Security considerations are built into design, development, testing, and release work through early information security involvement, use of approved tools or libraries, and automated checks that identify common vulnerabilities before software is deployed.
description: Pervasive security means security is treated as part of everyday software delivery work rather than as a final check after development. Teams involve information security specialists early in design, use approved libraries and tools, and build security checks into automated testing and delivery workflows. Security feedback appears throughout design, development, test, demos, and release activities instead of arriving only at the end. The capability is visible when major features receive early security review, common controls are standardized, and automated checks continuously test security requirements. In practice, security responsibilities are shared across development, QA, operations, and information security roles.

guidance.text: Start by making security visible in normal delivery work instead of leaving it to a late review.
