Identify major features that currently reach development or testing without any information security input, and add an early design review for them. Ask the information security function to define a small set of mandatory checkpoints for design, code, test, and release. Measure how many features receive early security review so the next step is concrete.

Move security engagement earlier and make it repeatable. Add information security participation at the start of design for new features, and invite them to relevant demos so issues surface before release. Replace ad hoc developer choices with a first set of approved libraries, packages, or toolchains for common tasks. Begin basic training on common security risks so teams can apply those standards consistently.

Reduce dependence on end-of-cycle security checks. Convert recurring manual review points into automated tests in the build or continuous integration process, starting with common vulnerabilities and required use of approved components. Track review lead time so security checks do not become an unmanaged source of delay. Expand measurement beyond design reviews by checking where security input is present across design, development, test, and release.

Standardize the way security is built into daily work. Ensure most major features receive early security review, and make approved libraries and toolchains the default path for delivery teams. Increase information security involvement in writing or reviewing automated security tests so coverage reflects actual security requirements. Review where defects are still being found late and add checks earlier in the lifecycle.

Broaden security integration across the full value stream. Include information security feedback in design reviews, automated tests, software demos, and release readiness so the same expectations appear at each stage. Measure the percentage of security requirements covered by automation and the actual use of approved tools, then target the largest gaps. Remove local exceptions that bypass the standard approach unless they are explicitly reviewed.

Make the capability reliable at scale. Use automated controls and tests to generate evidence on demand that required security checks are running, and review that evidence with the people who depend on it. Keep reducing the elapsed time added by security review while maintaining early involvement and automated coverage. Refresh approved libraries, packages, and toolchains as teams learn where developers still need custom or manual work.

Protect the habits that make security pervasive. Continue tracking early review coverage, lifecycle involvement, automated security coverage, review time, and use of approved tools so regression is visible quickly. Revisit tests, standards, and approved components as systems and risks evolve, and keep information security engaged in design and demos rather than only during exceptions. When new work patterns appear, fold them into the same security workflow instead of creating parallel processes.

name: Platform engineering
question: Day-to-day software delivery is supported by a shared internal platform that lets team members complete common tasks through clear, self-service workflows, with consistent feedback and minimal reliance on ticket-based help for infrastructure, deployment, testing, or operational setup.
description: Platform engineering is the capability to provide software teams with a shared internal platform that makes common delivery work easier, safer, and more consistent. In practice, this means routine tasks such as setting up services, testing changes, deploying software, and handling operational needs can be completed through standardized self-service workflows instead of ad hoc requests. The platform reduces the amount of infrastructure, security, and operational detail that individual developers must manage directly. It also gives clear feedback when tasks succeed or fail, so people can continue work and troubleshoot without depending on a ticket queue. The capability is visible when teams can deliver through repeatable platform paths that are easy to use across projects.

guidance.text: Most common delivery work still depends on manual setup, specialist knowledge, or ticket queues. Start by selecting one frequent workflow, such as creating a service or deploying a change, and map every step, handoff, and delay. Build a minimal self-service path for that workflow and make the result visible to the people who use it.

Some shared tooling exists, but it does not yet remove much friction from daily work. Stop adding platform features based only on assumptions, and gather direct input from developers about the hardest steps in the chosen workflow. Use that input to set a short roadmap and improve one end-to-end journey rather than several disconnected tools.

A usable platform path exists for a few tasks, but team members still need deep infrastructure or policy knowledge to complete them. Move more of that complexity into platform defaults, templates, and automated checks so common tasks can be completed with fewer decisions. Add clear task outcomes, logs, and diagnostics so people can understand failures without waiting for manual support.

Common workflows are available through the platform, but the experience is still uneven and adoption is not yet reliable. Measure task success, onboarding and continued use, and software delivery outcomes for the workflows the platform supports. Use those signals to remove steps that still force handoffs, rework, or side-channel help.

The platform is helping delivery work, but it risks becoming a bottleneck if only one team can change it. Define clear APIs, extension points, and contribution rules so other teams can add services and capabilities in a consistent way. Keep the core workflow opinionated where it reduces complexity, while allowing controlled flexibility for teams with different technical needs.

The platform is broadly useful and supports many day-to-day tasks, but sustained improvement now depends on disciplined feedback loops. Review delivery metrics together with developer sentiment to spot cases where convenience is improving but throughput, stability, or recovery are not. Strengthen feedback on task outcomes and prioritize fixes that let teams troubleshoot, recover, and continue work without waiting on the platform team.

The platform operates as a reliable internal product that consistently reduces friction in software delivery. Sustain this level by regularly revisiting user journeys, refreshing the roadmap from developer needs, and monitoring adoption, task success, delivery performance, and sentiment together. Avoid regression by resisting ticket-driven operating modes, rigid top-down standards, and large all-at-once releases that outpace real team workflows.

name: Proactive failure notification
question: Notifications are generated from well-chosen thresholds and rate-of-change signals, reach the people able to act, and are regularly reviewed so issues are detected and addressed before failures are noticed by external teams or customers.
description: Proactive failure notification means using monitoring data to warn teams when system behavior is moving toward failure, instead of waiting until a service is already down or someone outside the team reports it. In practice, this shows up as alerting rules tied to meaningful thresholds and change patterns that indicate likely user impact. The capability also includes sending notifications through channels that reach people who can respond, while avoiding large volumes of alerts that do not lead to action. Teams demonstrating this capability use incident learning to refine what they monitor and treat notifications as an operational signal for early diagnosis and response.

guidance.text: Start by identifying a small set of critical services and the metrics most likely to show emerging failure. Create basic alerting rules for those metrics instead of relying on ad hoc detection or external reports. Route each notification to a person or team that can take action. Review recent incidents and note which signals would have shown trouble earlier.

Replace generic or arbitrary alerts with thresholds linked to actual service risk. For each alert, define what condition triggers it, who receives it, and what action is expected. Remove notifications that never lead to action. Add at least one early warning that signals deterioration before a full failure occurs.

Strengthen coverage by checking whether monitored thresholds reflect points where users begin to feel impact. Add rate-of-change monitoring where sudden movement matters, not just absolute values. Make notification channels explicit so alerts do not depend on informal handoffs. Use post-incident review notes to add missing indicators into monitoring.

Tune alerts so they provide earlier and more reliable warning without creating noise. Compare recent notifications against actual incidents to see which alerts predicted real trouble and which did not. Automate responses for recurring cases that require the same action every time. Reduce duplicate or low-value notifications that distract during active events.

Make alert review a regular team practice rather than an occasional cleanup task. Check whether notifications consistently reach the people who can diagnose and fix the problem quickly. Monitor key system health indicators in more than one way, such as threshold warnings and rate-of-change warnings. Use incident post-mortems to update alert rules, thresholds, and routing based on observed failure patterns.

Measure whether notifications are arriving early enough to prevent escalation to external operations groups or customers. Refine thresholds using evidence about when user-facing impact begins, and trigger warnings before that point is crossed. Watch for alert fatigue by tracking notification volume during incidents and removing alerts that cannot be acted upon. Strengthen automation and routing so human attention is reserved for signals that need judgment.

Sustain performance by continuously validating that alerts still predict meaningful issues as systems, traffic patterns, and user expectations change. Keep post-mortem findings tightly connected to monitoring updates so new failure indicators are captured quickly. Regularly prune non-actionable notifications and verify that automated responses still behave correctly. Guard against regression by reviewing whether important problems are still detected internally before they are reported by external teams or customers.

name: Streamlining change approval
question: Changes are typically reviewed and approved by peers within the development workflow, supported by automated checks and clear approval steps, so that most changes move forward quickly, consistently, and with little waiting for external review bodies.
description: Streamlining change approval means handling routine software changes through the normal development workflow instead of sending them through separate, heavyweight approval steps. In practice, approval is usually shown through peer review recorded in the development platform, so someone other than the author checks the change before it moves forward. Automated testing, integration, monitoring, and observability are used to reveal defects, security issues, performance problems, and stability risks early. The process is also understandable to team members, who know how a change moves from submission to acceptance and what approvals are required. Respondents should think about whether change approval is fast, visible, and built into daily delivery work rather than delayed by external reviewers.

guidance.text: Map the current approval path for a typical code change and record every handoff, reviewer, and queue. Stop relying on informal knowledge of who approves what, and document the steps from submitted to accepted for the change types the team makes most often. Begin capturing approvals in the development platform so there is visible evidence that someone other than the author reviewed the change.

Introduce peer review for routine changes at code check-in time instead of sending most changes to an external approval body. Require review comments and approvals to be stored with the change so segregation of duties is visible and auditable. Measure how long changes wait for approval and identify the most common delays.

Add automated tests to the normal path for each change so obvious regressions are found before deployment. Strengthen continuous integration so reviews and automated checks happen together and teams get feedback quickly after commit. Use the approval data and test results to separate routine issues from changes that truly need extra attention.

Start treating changes differently based on risk instead of sending every change through the same process. Define which signals make a change high risk, such as security, performance, or stability impact, and flag those changes early for additional scrutiny. Let lower-risk changes move through peer review and automated checks without extra manual waiting.

Look at the approval process end to end and remove bottlenecks that keep changes waiting outside the development workflow. Shift validations into the platform and toolchain where possible, including security and other controls that can be checked automatically. Track lead time, waiting time for approval, and change fail rate so teams can see whether the process is becoming faster and safer.

Use monitoring and observability after changes are committed so teams can detect and correct bad changes quickly in production. Make the regular approval path reliable enough that the same mechanism can be used for emergency changes instead of creating separate exceptions. Keep the role of leadership and change boards focused on coordination and business trade-offs rather than detailed gatekeeping.

Protect the capability by reviewing measures regularly, especially waiting time for approvals, use of external approvals, and outcomes for different risk profiles. Continue improving the platform so developers receive fast feedback on security, performance, stability, and defects without adding heavyweight manual steps. Watch for regression patterns, such as treating all changes the same or responding to incidents by adding extra approvals, and correct them quickly.

name: Team experimentation
question: Team members can pursue new ideas, adjust stories and specifications during development, and make those changes without needing approval from outside the team, while using what is learned from users and the problem to shape the solution.
description: Team experimentation means software teams are trusted to explore new ideas and shape solutions as they learn more during delivery. Work begins from the outcome to achieve or problem to solve, rather than treating requirements as fixed instructions that cannot be changed. In practice, teams can prototype, test, and revise stories or specifications based on what they discover about users, the product, and the work itself. The capability is visible when teams make informed decisions about what to build and how to change it without relying on external approval for routine adjustments.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where approval from outside the team blocks changes to stories, specifications, or new ideas. Create one small area of work where the team can make limited changes during development without asking for permission first. Give the team the business outcome or problem statement for that work so decisions are tied to purpose rather than fixed instructions. Track how often external permission is still needed and remove one common blocker next.

Stop treating stories as complete instructions that cannot change once implementation begins. Introduce short conversations at the start of work to clarify the problem to solve, the expected outcome, and where team judgment can be used. Allow the team to make minor specification updates during development and record what changed and why. Measure how often useful changes are delayed because someone outside the team must approve them.

Strengthen the team's ability to test ideas quickly by creating simple ways to prototype and gather feedback during development. Provide access to the context needed for decisions, such as expected business outcomes, known customer problems, and relevant feedback. Define which kinds of story and specification changes the team can make independently as normal practice. Review whether these changes are helping the team respond to what is learned rather than continuing with outdated assumptions.

Expand experimentation from occasional exceptions into a standard part of delivery. Make it normal for teams to revise stories, specifications, and solution details when new evidence emerges during the work. Encourage developers to prototype and test ideas early enough that learning can still influence the design. Measure whether teams are able to work on new ideas and make changes without repeated external approval.

Increase the quality of team decisions by improving access to customer context and outcome information. Create regular opportunities for developers and operators to observe or talk to customers so problem understanding does not depend only on written requirements. Encourage iteration when the first solution is not sufficient, and check that teams are using what they learn to refine the product or service. Look for patterns in permission requests that still slow down independent changes and remove them.

Strengthen autonomy so experimentation covers both what is built and how the work is carried out. Leave more implementation details to the people doing the work, including changes to stories, specifications, and technical approaches when they are appropriate. Use regular team practices such as hackathons or dedicated experiment time to surface and test new ideas in a quick, low-friction way. Measure whether learning from these experiments is consistently incorporated into product or service design.

Sustain performance by protecting the team's freedom to pursue new ideas and make informed changes without reverting to outside approval for routine decisions. Keep outcome and customer information visible so experimentation remains grounded in real problems and not isolated technical activity. Continue reviewing whether teams can still prototype, test, and revise work quickly as complexity, scale, or governance demands increase. Watch for signs that stories are becoming fixed instructions again and correct that regression early.

name: Test automation
question: Automated unit and acceptance tests are created and maintained by developers, run on every change in the delivery pipeline, provide feedback within minutes, and are complemented by ongoing manual exploratory or usability testing during development.
description: Test automation means validating software continuously throughout delivery instead of waiting for a separate testing phase at the end. In practice, teams use automated unit and acceptance tests in the delivery pipeline, while also continuing manual activities such as exploratory, usability, and acceptance testing during development. Developers are directly involved in writing and maintaining tests, and testers work alongside them to shape coverage and explore product behavior. The test suites are kept fast, reliable, and relevant so that changes get feedback within minutes and failures point to real defects. This capability is visible when every change is checked through repeatable tests that support release decisions and expose problems early.

guidance.text: Start by putting basic testing into the normal delivery flow instead of waiting until work is considered complete. Create a small skeleton pipeline with one unit test, one acceptance test, and an automated deployment step that produces an environment for exploratory testing. Make every code change trigger this basic flow so feedback starts arriving during development rather than after handoff. Pair testers and developers early so testing knowledge is built into the work from the start.

Expand beyond isolated tests and make testing a continuous activity across the lifecycle. Require developers to add unit and acceptance tests for new or changed functionality, and keep manual exploratory, usability, and acceptance testing active during development rather than at the end. Measure how long automated feedback takes on local machines and in CI, then remove obvious delays so feedback becomes usable for daily decisions. The next step is to make these tests part of the main delivery workflow, not optional side activities.

Make developers the primary authors and maintainers of automated tests so failures can be fixed where the change was made. Treat automated acceptance tests as part of development, and do not consider work complete unless those tests pass. When a bug is found in acceptance testing, exploratory testing, or production, add automated coverage that would catch the same problem earlier next time. This shifts defect discovery toward cheaper and faster feedback points.

Curate the test suites so they stay relevant as the product changes. Review whether acceptance tests represent real end-to-end user journeys and business checks, rather than becoming a loose collection of scripted criteria. Prune redundant or over-mocked unit tests, and refactor brittle UI tests by decoupling them from frequent interface changes. Build testability improvements into daily refactoring work so the suites become easier to maintain as the system evolves.

Increase trust in the pipeline by making automated tests reliable and meaningful. Stop tolerating flaky tests and poorly coded failures, because a failed test should indicate a real defect and a passing suite should support release confidence. Rebalance the suite so fast unit tests catch most defects before slower acceptance tests or manual checks are needed. Also track and reduce the time spent fixing acceptance test failures so developers can recover quickly when the pipeline breaks.

Use explicit measures to tune the testing system instead of relying on general impressions. Track who writes and maintains unit and acceptance tests, where bugs are found over time, how often automated failures represent real defects, and whether all suites run on every pipeline trigger. Use those measures to move defect discovery into earlier, cheaper test phases and to close gaps where tests are skipped or slow to repair. Keep testers and developers jointly reviewing the data so coverage and workflow improve together.

Sustain performance by protecting the practices that keep feedback fast, trustworthy, and continuous. Keep all relevant suites running on every pipeline trigger, preserve short feedback times on local and CI workflows, and continue collaboration between testers and developers on both automation and exploratory testing. When defects escape late or release confidence drops, update the pipeline by adding or refining tests that catch those problems earlier. Watch for regression signals such as broken pipelines, flaky tests, rising repair time for test failures, or more bugs appearing in late testing and production.

name: Test data management
question: Test suites can obtain realistic, current, and appropriately scoped data when needed, without depending on shared or outdated datasets, and automated tests usually create or control the data and state they require to run reliably in parallel.
description: Test data management is the practice of ensuring automated tests have access to realistic, relevant data without creating unnecessary dependency on fragile or risky data sources. In practice, teams can run their full automated suites because the required data is available, current, and suitable for the scenarios being tested. Tests obtain or create needed data on demand rather than waiting for manual preparation or depending on shared state left behind by other tests. Teams keep test data closely tied to specific tests, limit reliance on full production copies or persistent databases, and protect sensitive information. This capability is visible when automated tests are not blocked, slowed, or narrowed by missing, stale, or unsafe data sources.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where automated tests depend on missing, shared, or outdated data. Replace ad hoc use of full production copies with a small set of relevant, non-sensitive datasets for the most important test scenarios. Assign ownership for refreshing and publishing those datasets so they are easy to find. Measure how much time is spent preparing or fixing data before tests can run.

Reduce unnecessary data dependence in unit tests. Rewrite unit tests so they do not require external data or state outside the test scope. Separate tests that truly need shared data from those that can build their own inputs locally. Track which failures come from data setup rather than code behavior and prioritize removing those dependencies.

Make baseline test data available on demand instead of through manual requests or one-off scripts. Version the datasets used by automated suites and refresh them regularly so they stay relevant as APIs and interfaces change. Stop reusing stale data whose purpose is unclear or no longer matches current scenarios. Confirm that key user journeys and important edge cases each have a defined data source.

Strengthen isolation so each test uses data explicitly associated with that test. Have tests create the state they need during setup, preferably through application APIs, and reset or discard it after use. Remove shared mutable datasets that let one test influence another. Check whether isolated data now allows more tests to run reliably in parallel.

Reduce dependence on persistent database fixtures where they are not essential. Use in-memory or more local data stores when appropriate, and keep database-backed data limited to the minimum needed for realistic higher-level tests. Export only the relevant sections of data instead of cloning whole production databases. Measure how many automated tests can run without acquiring additional data beyond the standard test setup.

Make self-service access to key datasets routine for the full automated suite. Refresh those datasets on a predictable schedule, and monitor access frequency so gaps in availability are visible early. Mask or hash sensitive information in any data derived from production-like sources. Review whether missing data still prevents certain scenarios, environments, or parallel runs, and close those gaps systematically.

Sustain the capability by keeping test data small, current, relevant, and easy for automated suites to acquire on demand. Watch for regression signals such as rising time spent manipulating data, renewed use of full production copies, or more tests depending on shared database state. Periodically confirm that tests still control their own setup and can run in parallel without data collisions. Continue pruning obsolete datasets and updating representative ones as interfaces and user journeys change.

name: Transformational leadership
question: People who influence this software project provide a clear direction, communicate it consistently, encourage new ways to solve delivery problems, show consideration for team members, and recognize behaviors that support adoption of effective engineering and product practices.
description: Transformational leadership in DORA describes leadership behaviors that help software teams improve delivery by enabling adoption of effective technical and product management practices, rather than trying to drive results through direct control alone. In practice, team members observe a clear direction, encouraging communication about change, questions that challenge old assumptions, consideration for personal needs, and recognition of meaningful contributions. The capability is not limited to executives or managers; it can be shown by anyone who influences the work and shapes daily interactions. It is visible in concrete behaviors such as setting understandable goals, helping remove obstacles, reinforcing useful experiments and cross-team help, and staying consistent when the team is under stress. Respondents should think about whether these behaviors are regularly experienced around the project, because DORA measures the capability through what team members observe leaders doing.

guidance.text: Leadership behaviors are mostly absent or experienced as one-way instruction. Start by naming the project direction and one or two measurable delivery goals that can be repeated consistently in routine conversations. Replace compliance-focused messaging with questions about obstacles, failed past attempts, and ideas to try next, and begin recognizing specific helpful behaviors such as experiments and cross-team support.

Some positive behaviors appear, but they are irregular and easy to miss. Turn the direction into language that team members can repeat without looking it up, and restate it in planning, standups, and reviews. Add a regular forum where influential people ask what is blocking improvement, what was learned from previous attempts, and which next experiment deserves support and recognition.

A basic direction exists, but support and stimulation are inconsistent across day-to-day work. Before changes are made, explicitly consider personal needs and likely stress points, and make time for concerns to surface. Use one current delivery problem to practice rethinking assumptions and choosing a different approach, then acknowledge the people who contribute useful ideas or learning.

Leadership behaviors are visible and sometimes helpful, but they are not yet tightly connected to practice adoption. Use the stated goals and conversations to remove concrete barriers to the technical and product practices the team is trying to adopt. Make recognition more deliberate by praising behaviors that move these practices forward, including experiments that fail but produce useful learning.

The capability is strong in many interactions, but it can still depend on a few individuals or a few situations. Spread the behaviors by asking senior engineers, product managers, and other influencers to model clear direction, supportive questions, and specific recognition in their own daily interactions. Check whether different team members describe the same vision and can point to recent examples of obstacle removal, experiments, or help given to other teams.

Transformational leadership is embedded, and the next step is to make it consistently effective under changing conditions. Review at a regular cadence whether delivery goals are still clear, obstacles are surfaced early, and recognition is reinforcing learning, experimentation, and help across team boundaries. Watch for drift toward directive behavior during incidents or deadlines, and correct it quickly by returning to clear communication, thoughtful support, and inquiry.

The capability is sustained and broadly visible across the project. Preserve it by continuing clear vision, inspirational communication, intellectual stimulation, supportive behavior, and personal recognition in normal work and under stress. Periodically test whether people can describe the direction consistently and cite recent examples of obstacle removal, thoughtful support, and recognition tied to learning, and avoid regression to instruction-and-compliance management.

name: Trunk-based development
question: Code changes are usually developed in small batches, merged into trunk at least daily, reviewed without long delays, and supported by automated tests so the shared branch remains in a working state.
description: Trunk-based development means team members integrate code into the shared main line in small, frequent changes instead of working for days or weeks on long-lived branches. In practice, work is split into batches that can be merged at least daily, often several times a day, so integration happens continuously rather than being delayed. The shared branch is expected to stay usable, which depends on automated tests around each commit and quick action when a build fails. Observable signs include few active branches, little or no code freeze time, and code review that does not leave changes waiting for long periods. This capability reflects how consistently a team keeps code current and avoids large, complex merge events.

guidance.text: Work is mostly done on long-lived branches, and merging into trunk happens late or only after large pieces are finished. Start by tracking how many active branches exist, how often code is merged, and how much time is spent in freezes or stabilization. Pick one repository or workflow and require smaller change sets that can be merged sooner. Also define a clear expectation that a broken trunk is fixed or reverted immediately instead of being left for later.

Some smaller merges happen, but many tasks still sit on branches for several days and review queues slow integration. Reduce batch size by breaking work into increments that are complete enough to merge without waiting for the whole feature. Set a same-day expectation for review and measure how long approvals take. Require tests to run before commit or merge so changes reach trunk with less ceremony and less risk.

Daily merging happens in parts of the team, but long-lived branches, integration phases, or code freezes still appear during larger work. Make daily merge frequency an explicit operating rule and visibly track branches that stay open too long. Cap the number of active branches in the repository and reduce exceptions. Run a fast automated test suite on every commit and stop new work to fix or revert changes when CI fails.

Trunk is used for much day-to-day development, yet the pattern is not consistent under pressure. Replace waiting-based review queues with synchronous reviews at commit time where possible, so changes do not sit idle and grow stale. Merge release-branch changes back to trunk quickly to avoid separate development lines drifting apart. Track approval time, branch age, and freeze time so delays become visible before they turn into larger merge problems.

Small-batch development and daily merges are normal, with few active branches and a working shared branch most of the time. Tighten the feedback loop by keeping build and test execution to a few minutes and protecting trunk with automated checks before changes land. Remove any remaining routine code freezes or dedicated integration phases, and use data to target outliers such as slow approvals or branches that remain open too long.

Trunk-based development is practiced consistently, with rapid reviews, short branch lifetime, and quick recovery when problems appear. Extend the same discipline across all repositories and contributors so exceptions do not create hidden long-lived branches or delayed merges. Strengthen coaching on slicing work into mergeable batches and keep release changes flowing back to trunk promptly so extra development lines do not accumulate.

Trunk is the clear current line of development, changes are small and frequent, and the shared branch stays usable through fast tests and immediate fixes. Sustain this by continuing to measure active branches, daily merge frequency, code freeze time, and approval delay, and by acting quickly when trends worsen. Preserve synchronous review habits, quick fix-or-revert decisions, and coaching on small-batch work so long-lived branches and stabilization phases do not return.

name: User-centric focus
question: Day-to-day work in this software project team is guided by a clear understanding of user needs, with direct feedback, user research, and user-focused metrics regularly used to prioritize work and evaluate whether delivered changes create value.
description: User-centric focus means software work is guided by a clear understanding of user needs and the value users should receive from the product. Teams with this capability make user experience a visible factor in planning, development, and evaluation, rather than treating it as a separate activity. In practice, this shows up when developers, product roles, and researchers use direct feedback, observation, and usage data to shape priorities. It is also visible when teams track user-oriented measures such as satisfaction, adoption, or task success alongside delivery metrics. The capability reflects whether everyday decisions stay connected to real user problems and whether feedback changes what gets built next.

guidance.text: User needs are largely absent from daily development decisions. Start by identifying the main user groups and the problems the product is expected to solve for them. Create at least one direct feedback channel, such as short in-app prompts or observation sessions, and make the input visible to the team. Stop treating velocity or shipped features as the only signals of progress.

Some user information exists, but it is inconsistent, delayed, or filtered through too many handoffs. Strengthen the connection by reviewing fresh feedback on a regular cadence and linking it to current backlog items. Involve engineers in selected research or testing sessions so they can observe user behavior directly. Begin tracking one or two user outcome measures alongside delivery metrics.

User input influences some decisions, but not yet in a reliable, team-wide way. Define how feedback, research findings, and analytics should trigger backlog changes or specification updates. Make user metrics visible in the same places where the team reviews delivery status. Reduce reliance on secondhand summaries when direct exposure to users is possible.

The team considers users in planning and review, but the connection is still uneven across features or roles. Strengthen consistency by expecting new work to show the user problem, the intended outcome, and the evidence behind it. Use direct feedback and observed user behavior to confirm whether delivered changes improved task success, satisfaction, or adoption. Check whether AI-assisted output is being validated against real user requirements rather than generic patterns.

User needs are regularly considered and visible in team practices. Improve further by shortening the time between collecting feedback and changing priorities, so the team can respond while context is still fresh. Expand engineer participation in research, testing, or observation to deepen shared understanding. Use specifications to capture user needs and constraints clearly before implementation when AI tooling is involved.

User-centric behavior is strong and usually shapes what gets built. To reach the next level, tighten the link between user signals and measurable product outcomes such as retention, adoption, satisfaction, or task completion. Look for places where teams still default to shipping activity instead of user value, and remove those incentives. Standardize how feedback leads to reprioritization so the practice survives staffing changes and delivery pressure.

User needs consistently guide planning, implementation, and evaluation across the team. Sustain this level by keeping feedback channels low-latency, maintaining visible user metrics, and continuing direct engineer exposure to user research. Guard against regression into feature-factory behavior by reviewing whether shipped work changed user outcomes, not just output. When using AI, keep specifications and validation tied to real user requirements so faster delivery does not drift away from user value.

name: Version control
question: Version control is used consistently to store and track application code, configuration, infrastructure definitions, tests, deployment assets, and related artifacts, with changes committed in small, reviewable increments so current and past system states can be reproduced and traced.
description: This capability means teams use version control as the shared system of record for the artifacts needed to build, test, deploy, and operate software. In practice, that includes not only source code, but also configuration, infrastructure definitions, scripts, tests, deployment assets, dependencies, and other items required to recreate environments and delivery workflows. Day to day, teams can inspect what changed, identify which versions make up a given environment, compare states over time, and restore a previous known state when needed. The practical value is reproducibility, traceability, auditability, and faster recovery when defects, failures, or security issues occur.

guidance.text: Begin by identifying which artifacts are currently outside version control, starting with application code, deployment scripts, environment definitions, and test assets. Put them into a shared repository with basic access for the people who maintain and use them. Stop relying on local-only copies, shared drives, or manual handoffs as the primary source of truth. Measure simple coverage first, such as which artifact types are versioned and which are not.

Expand version control beyond source code so more of the delivery process can be reconstructed from what is stored there. Add the scripts, configuration files, dependency definitions, and operational assets that are used regularly but still live elsewhere. Create a clear repository structure and naming approach so people can find and update the right items consistently. Check whether a team member can locate the files needed to recreate a basic environment without asking around.

Strengthen traceability by making changes easier to understand and relate to specific system states. Encourage smaller, cleaner commits that group related changes and make review and rollback easier. Start tagging or otherwise labeling versions that correspond to important builds, releases, or environment states. Verify that someone can compare two versions of an environment and explain what changed between them.

Use version-controlled assets to recreate more of the build, test, and deployment workflow instead of treating the repository as passive storage. Ensure the scripts and definitions required to package software, provision environments, and run tests are actually used from version control during normal work. Reduce dependence on undocumented manual steps that break reproducibility. Measure whether a production-like environment can be recreated using only the stored scripts and configuration.

Increase reliability by making recovery and rollback routine, not exceptional. Make sure previous known states can be identified quickly and restored with the exact versions of code, configuration, and dependencies that produced them. Add any missing artifacts that still prevent predictable re-creation of environments or releases. Track how long it takes to restore service or recreate an environment from version-controlled assets.

Refine day-to-day working habits so version history stays useful as change volume grows. Strengthen the practice of making small, frequent, reviewable commits at clean states rather than delaying commits until large work is complete. Version AI-related artifacts, such as prompts and agent configuration files, if they affect how software is produced or changed. Watch for recurring merge conflicts, delayed commits, or missing artifacts as signals that the workflow is weakening.

Sustain performance by regularly checking that version control still covers everything required to build, test, deploy, recover, and audit the system. Review new tools, platforms, dependencies, and AI-assisted workflows to ensure their key artifacts are added before gaps appear. Keep measuring commit frequency, recovery capability, rollback readiness, and artifact coverage so regressions are visible early. When exceptions or manual workarounds appear, remove them quickly so reproducibility and traceability do not erode over time.

name: Visibility of work in the value stream
question: Team members can see how work moves from idea to customer, access shared visual displays showing current status across upstream and downstream stages, and review flow metrics such as lead time and rework in day-to-day work.
description: Visibility of work in the value stream means that software teams can see how work moves from idea through development and release to customer use. In practice, this includes knowing the current state of products, features, and work items across upstream and downstream stages, not only inside a single team. The capability is visible when workflow information is shown in shared boards, dashboards, or similar displays that make handoffs, status, and responsibilities clear. It also involves having factual flow data available, such as lead time, process time, rework, and related delivery measures, so the state of work is based on actual conditions. The focus is on end-to-end visibility of product development work across the value stream.

guidance.text: Start by making current work visible for the part of the process the team directly handles. Create a simple visual display that shows work items, their current stage, and where they came from or go next. Begin recording actual elapsed time and obvious rework instead of relying on memory or opinion. Review the display regularly so it reflects the real current state of work.

Expand visibility beyond internal development tasks to include how work enters the team and how it is handed off downstream. Bring in representatives from adjacent groups to agree on the main process steps and the responsibilities at each step. Capture lead time and process time for recent work using actual examples rather than estimates. Make the visual display accessible to everyone involved in this part of the flow.

Create a current-state value stream map that covers several connected stages instead of only one team’s workflow. Break the stream into clear process blocks and record the activity performed and the team responsible for each block. Add a measure for how often downstream work arrives complete and usable without rework. Use the map to identify where waiting and rework are concentrated before changing unrelated areas.

Turn visibility into an ongoing practice, not a one-time workshop output. Keep a dynamic board or dashboard updated as work changes so current status can be seen at any time. Add statistical data such as lead time, deploy frequency, and percent complete and accurate so flow can be discussed with evidence. Use these views to focus attention on bottlenecks where lead time is much longer than process time.

Broaden the view to the full value stream from idea through operations and support. Include people from business, design, testing, QA, operations, and support in regular mapping and review sessions so the whole flow is represented. Create a future-state map with agreed changes and confirm that the people involved can actually make those changes. Re-run the exercise on a regular schedule and check whether targeted waiting and rework points are improving.

Make end-to-end visibility widely available and routinely used for decisions. Share current maps, boards, and related artifacts broadly so people across the organization can see the flow of product development work and its present state. Compare lead time, process time, and percent complete and accurate by process block to identify where poor upstream quality creates downstream rework. Stop prioritizing local efficiency changes that do not improve overall flow through the value stream.

Sustain performance by keeping current or recent value stream maps available and keeping live visual displays accurate and accessible. Continue measuring actual flow data on a regular cadence and update responsibilities, stages, and metrics when the value stream changes. Use reviews to detect new bottlenecks, hidden handoffs, or rising rework before they become normal. Maintain cross-functional participation and decision-making authority so visibility remains end to end rather than drifting back to isolated team views.

name: Visual management
question: Key information about work status, build and deployment health, and important operational outcomes is displayed in a clear, current, easy-to-understand way, and the team regularly uses it during daily work to notice issues and act on them.
description: Visual management means making important information about software delivery visible in places where team members can quickly see and use it during everyday work. In practice, this includes showing the current state of work, build and pipeline health, flow through the backlog, and relevant production signals in a form that is easy to understand at a glance. The capability is present when the displayed information matches what the team actually cares about, stays current, and helps people spot obstacles or abnormal conditions early. It also means the displays are part of regular working routines rather than passive status artifacts that nobody uses. The core idea is shared visibility that supports timely action on real delivery and operational issues.

guidance.text: Start by making a small set of delivery and operational information visible to everyone in the team area or shared workspace. Choose only a few items that can be checked every day, such as work in progress, build status, deployment pipeline state, or a small number of production signals. Keep the display simple enough that a person can understand it in seconds. Agree on who updates each part and when it must be refreshed.

Review whether the current display contains information that team members actually use to make decisions. Remove stale, decorative, or low-value metrics that create noise but do not lead to action. Replace them with indicators tied to current goals, active constraints, or common delivery problems. Make updates part of daily work so the information is no longer ignored as outdated.

Strengthen the connection between the display and daily team routines. Use the board, dashboard, or monitor during standups, work selection, and problem investigation so issues are noticed and discussed in real time. Make sure each indicator shows what needs attention and what follow-up is expected when it changes. Add a clear path from noticing a problem to diagnosing it further.

Refine the display so it is easy to understand at a glance, even from a distance or in a quick scan of a virtual workspace. Simplify layouts, labels, and signals so the important information stands out immediately. Check whether the display is helping identify blocked work, failing stages, or production problems early enough to matter. Measure whether people are actually acting when the display shows a problem.

Evolve the display to reflect the team's current context instead of keeping the same metrics indefinitely. Remove indicators that no longer help and introduce new ones when goals, risks, or constraints change. Use retrospectives to ask whether the display is current, actionable, and contributing to measurable progress. Treat the display as a working tool that changes as obstacles are removed and new ones appear.

Use the display to address underlying system problems rather than only fixing visible symptoms. When an indicator turns red or shows a negative trend, trace the issue to its root cause and capture the constraint that keeps it recurring. Include information that helps the team connect delivery flow, pipeline health, and production behavior to broader outcomes. Strengthen the habit of responding with problem solving, not metric management.

Sustain the capability by regularly checking that every visible indicator still matters, stays current, and leads to action when needed. Continue reviewing the display in retrospectives and remove anything that has become noise, inaccurate, or disconnected from team goals. Watch for regression into stale boards, overly complex dashboards, or pressure to optimize the display instead of the underlying system. Preserve simple, well-understood visuals and keep adapting them as the work and constraints change.

name: Well-being
question: Day-to-day work allows team members to spend most time on planned value-adding work, experience low stress and disruption during production deployments, and work in an environment that actively reduces common burnout risks.
description: Well-being in this context reflects whether software delivery work supports job satisfaction and sustainable performance rather than creating chronic stress. It is visible in how production deployments feel in practice, whether they are routine and low-disruption or anxiety-inducing events. It also shows up in how much team time goes to planned feature and infrastructure work versus unplanned fixes, patches, defects, audit requests, and customer-support issues. The capability includes whether the work environment avoids common burnout risks such as overload, low control, weak support, unfair decisions, and conflicts between organizational and individual values. Taken together, it describes whether everyday delivery practices let people create value without excessive fear, rework, or exhaustion.

guidance.text: Production work is likely experienced as disruptive, reactive, and draining. Start by making the problem visible: record when deployments cause fear, disruption, or emergency follow-up work, and estimate how much time is spent on new work versus rework. List the main sources of unplanned work, such as break-fix tasks, patches, defect response, audit requests, and customer support. Ask team members which burnout risk factors are most present in the work environment so the next changes target the environment instead of individuals.

Some pain points are visible, but they are not yet managed consistently. Review recent production changes and identify the specific steps that make deployments stressful or disruptive. Begin classifying effort into proactive new work and reactive unplanned work on a regular cadence. Use that data to pick one recurring source of rework and one workplace risk factor, such as overload or lack of control, to address next.

The issues are being named, but the day-to-day experience is still uneven. Put technical practices in place that support continuous delivery so production changes become less disruptive and less anxiety-provoking. Protect regular time for planned feature, test, and infrastructure work instead of letting urgent fixes consume the schedule. Check whether the changes reduce emergency patches, break-fix tasks, and feelings of helplessness.

Deployments are becoming more manageable, and the balance of work is starting to improve. Track trends over time for deployment pain, unplanned work, defects found by end users, customer support work, and security remediation so quality problems are easier to spot. Give team members clearer influence over decisions that affect their work, and make decision processes more transparent and fair. Use these signals to remove the conditions that keep creating rework.

The team is showing consistent signs of healthier delivery, but weak spots still surface under pressure. Standardize the working patterns that keep deployments routine, and document how urgent patches and break-fix work are handled so they do not crowd out new work. Review workload, recognition, team support, fairness, and value conflicts as part of regular team management rather than only after problems escalate. Measure whether planned value-adding work is continuing to grow while reactive work is shrinking.

Well-being is strong in normal delivery, and improvement now depends on disciplined maintenance. Treat increases in deployment disruption, end-user defects, support load, or urgent audit and remediation work as early warnings that quality is slipping. Respond by adjusting the work environment quickly, for example by reducing overload or restoring team control where it has eroded. Keep checking that most effort is still going into structured new work rather than correction and recovery.

Well-being is deeply embedded in how software is delivered and managed. Sustain it by continuing to watch deployment pain, work mix, and burnout risk factors so regression is detected before it becomes normal. Preserve the technical practices that make deployments low-stress and the management practices that keep workload, control, fairness, community, rewards, and values aligned. Recheck these conditions whenever team structure, delivery pace, or support demands change.

name: Work in process limits
question: Work is visualized across the delivery flow, explicit work-in-process limits match available capacity, new tasks are pulled only when space opens, and delays or idle time are used to address bottlenecks rather than starting additional work.
description: This capability is about making current work visible and limiting how many items can be in progress at each step of delivery. Teams use a shared board that shows work across the value stream, including analysis, development, testing, and operations, so blocked or aging items can be seen early. Explicit limits are set for each stage based on actual team capacity, and new work is pulled only when an item moves forward. In practice, this means people avoid splitting attention across many tasks and instead complete a smaller number of higher-priority items. The capability is evident when teams treat waiting time and bottlenecks as signals to improve flow rather than as reasons to start more work.

guidance.text: Start by making all current work visible on one shared board instead of managing it through separate lists, messages, or memory. Represent each work item as a card and include the full flow needed to deliver it, not just development tasks. Stop starting new work until the board reflects what is already underway. The next step is to give the team a common view of where work is waiting, blocked, or aging.

Expand the board so it covers the whole value stream from idea to customer, including analysis, testing, operations, support work, and other work that competes for capacity. Define clear columns that show how work moves, and mark cards daily so blocked or slow-moving items become obvious. Introduce a regular prioritization routine so waiting work is ordered before anyone pulls the next item. The next step is to move from visibility alone to explicit control of how much work can be active at once.

Set a numeric work-in-process limit for each active stage, based on how many people or pairs can actually work there. Account for time consumed by production support, meetings, and technical debt so the limits reflect real capacity. Stop assigning enough simultaneous work to force people to split attention across multiple tasks. The next step is to follow the limits consistently and pull work only when space opens.

Use the board as a pull system: when a column is full, do not add another card until one moves forward. When people become idle because downstream work is blocked, resist raising the limit and instead help remove the constraint in that part of the flow. Make sure the next item pulled is the highest-priority card waiting in the queue. The next step is to use the limits to expose recurring delays and reduce hand-offs rather than treating the board as a tracking tool only.

Start measuring flow directly by recording when each item starts and when it finishes, then review mean lead time and variability across the full stream. Use aging cards, blocked work, and lead-time patterns to identify where the main delays occur. Simplify the delivery path by reducing unnecessary columns, hand-offs, and manual steps that slow movement between stages. The next step is to turn those observations into routine process changes that increase flow.

Run a weekly prioritization meeting with stakeholders so work enters the system in a clear order and low-priority items do not crowd active work. Use lead-time data to choose one or two constraints to address, such as slow environment preparation, excessive hand-offs, or tasks that could be automated. After an obstacle is reduced, lower limits again if flow feels too comfortable, so the next constraint becomes visible. The next step is to make this cycle of prioritizing, measuring, and process improvement a stable operating habit.

Keep the board accurate, the limits current, and the pull rules visible whenever capacity or support load changes. Continue reviewing mean lead time, variability, blocked work, and surfaced obstacles during retrospectives, and act quickly before delays become normal again. Avoid regression by resisting pressure to add hidden work, extra columns, or relaxed limits when demand spikes. Sustain performance by continuing to simplify, automate, and collaborate across the value stream while using single-piece flow as a guiding direction.

name: Working in small batches
question: Work is regularly broken into small, independent, testable changes that can be completed within hours or a few days, integrated into trunk, and released or dark launched without waiting for larger features or bundled downstream handoffs.
description: Working in small batches means structuring software changes as small, independent, testable units that can move through development, testing, and release quickly. In practice, teams decompose features so meaningful increments can be finished in hours or a few days instead of accumulating into large releases. Changes are integrated continuously, often directly to trunk, and incomplete functionality can be kept hidden through techniques such as dark launches or feature toggles. A batch is not really done when coding stops; it is done when it is deployed and feedback from tests, monitoring, quality checks, or users begins. This capability reflects whether day-to-day work is organized to shorten feedback cycles and reduce the need to bundle changes before validation.

guidance.text: Stop starting work that cannot be completed or validated in a reasonable slice. Take one upcoming feature and break it into independent pieces using a simple checklist: can it stand alone, be tested, and be released separately? Measure how long changes currently wait before integration or release so the next improvement target is visible.

Choose one stream of work and require batches that finish in a few days rather than large feature branches. Commit independent changes before the whole feature is complete, and make each batch testable before moving on. Track where batches are being regrouped for QA or release, because those handoffs are blocking faster feedback.

Adopt a consistent definition of a small batch so work is independent, valuable, estimable, and testable. Increase check-ins to trunk so developers integrate small releasable changes at least daily on selected work. For changes that depend on unfinished UI or downstream components, use dark launching or a simple feature toggle so deployment can happen without exposing incomplete functionality.

Make small-batch planning the default rather than something used only on easy stories. Review batches that take more than a week or require regrouping before test or release, then redesign them into smaller service, API, or behavior changes. Strengthen automated tests and monitoring so deployment to production can start the feedback process quickly and consistently.

Use MVP thinking for larger initiatives so product and business assumptions are tested through minimal slices instead of long delivery cycles. Measure how often releases are possible, what share of features finish within a week, and which larger items create delays. Use those measurements to target the remaining work types that still depend on long-lived branches or bundled releases.

Refine decomposition skills so most work can be completed in hours to a couple of days, checked into trunk, and deployed independently. Apply feature toggles, dark launching, or branch by abstraction deliberately when architecture changes would otherwise force batching. Compare feedback timing across automated tests, monitoring, QA, and user signals, then shorten the slowest paths.

Protect the discipline that keeps batches small by watching for regression into oversized pull requests, long-lived branches, or downstream bundling. Keep measuring release readiness, time to feedback, and the proportion of work completed within a week or less, and review outliers quickly. Continue strengthening decomposition skills and safe hidden-release techniques so higher delivery speed does not erode review, test, or integration quality.
